polls.text: Do you think that the problem statement is well-understood, solvable, and useful to solve?
Do you think there are real problems in this space to solve, butyour  are not sure if  MLS is the right starting point
Are you willing to work on clarification of the problem statement?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: The question I am trying to understand is as follows. I agree that MLS allows you to do unidirectional key updates. However, if you do a key update followed by actually using the new key and the update is lost, then obviously the recipient cannot decrypt the traffic. So how much survivability do you need in the case of that and is that a requirement of this work?

chat.author: Hyrum Mills
chat.text: difference in the url is that the full client is https://meetecho.ietf.org/client/?group=current and the lite client is https://meetecho.ietf.org/lite/?group=current

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text:
Eric Rescorla said:

The question I am trying to understand is as follows. I agree that MLS allows you to do unidirectional key updates. However, if you do a key update followed by actually using the new key and the update is lost, then obviously the recipient cannot decrypt the traffic. So how much survivability do you need in the case of that and is that a requirement of this work?

It's hard to imagine a recovery mechanism that didn't also create a vulnerability.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: In TLS, this is handled by having reliable and in-order delivery of the data and in DTLS/QUIC by requiring ACKs of the update packets before the new key is used

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:

Eric Rescorla said:

The question I am trying to understand is as follows. I agree that MLS allows you to do unidirectional key updates. However, if you do a key update followed by actually using the new key and the update is lost, then obviously the recipient cannot decrypt the traffic. So how much survivability do you need in the case of that and is that a requirement of this work?

It's hard to imagine a recovery mechanism that didn't also create a vulnerability.

Well hence my question :)

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: To elaborate about the situation with TLS 0-RTT a little bit, the issue with 0-RTT is basically twofold (1) You need to worry about two servers retaining state (2) you need to be able to handle the connection state being totally lost. And the way MLS handles this is by having hard synchronous state so that it just assumes neither of these can happen

chat.author: Benjamin Dowling

Britta Hale
chat.text:
Jonathan Hoyland said:

You might be able to use the keys of one in the other, but that doesn't, for example, prove that both parties agree on how the keys were derived. Maybe one party thinks it's just an OOB key but the other thinks it's from MLS

That is mixing handshake security model (which establishes binding) and channel security (which assumes it). It is no different from the TLS analyses that separate the two

An analysis that skips that binding is indeed leaving a hole ...

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: So I could talk a bit more about the drone swarm case, but the TL;DR is that the way you get into replay problems with TLS 1.3 0-RTT is that you have to deal with ambiguity about the state of the counterparty. MLS deals with this by just saying that there is a fixed state and not recover from it..

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: the "Drone Show" effort in ASTM F38 is supposedly single party.  At least for now!  Oh, and some need more than 1,000 nodes as was a limit mentioned in MLS session earlier today.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text:
Eric Rescorla said:

So I could talk a bit more about the drone swarm case, but the TL;DR is that the way you get into replay problems with TLS 1.3 0-RTT is that you have to deal with ambiguity about the state of the counterparty. MLS deals with this by just saying that there is a fixed state and not recover from it..

That's just a different design choice.  I'm not sure that either one is better.  I know that TLS was designed with the web in mind.  I don't know if fixed assumptions about the state of a peer is compatible with the way that the web works.

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: For BoF Question 4, I think the deliverables are not in the right order.  I think assuming a mulitparty protocol and designing for that makes more sene.  If there are sometimes only two parties, it is pretty likely to be a solvable change, where designs going the other direction are less likely to work.  I understand that this was a structural limitation to the BoF, but I still don't understand why.  As a result, I think this needs more thought.

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: Well, they need to coordinate, because if they aren't able to sequence IVs properly, the connection breaks and if they collide, the confidentiality and integrity goes away.

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: @EKR, and that is why I keep scratching my balding head on saying TLS with multi-party talks.

chat.author: John Preuß Mattsson
chat.text: My two-party problem statement for long-term connection. I would like TLS/DTLS/QUIC libraries that takes care of frequent rekeying and reauthentication providing Forward Secrecy (FS) and Post-Compromise Security (PCS). I do not want to change my application layer. That could as pointed out also be solved by CURRENT designing a layer on top of TLS/DTLS/QUIC. But such a solution does not solve the two-party space use cases.

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Martin Thomson said:

Well, they need to coordinate, because if they aren't able to sequence IVs properly, the connection breaks and if they collide, the confidentiality and integrity goes away.

The right way to deal with this is just to have different keys, obviously. Which is how TLS handles directional keys now

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: @Eric Rescorla There is also what some QUIC implementations do, with close coordination, which allows multiple senders to send into the same connection.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: I'm a big fan of channel bindings, so I think the analysis would be great fun.

I do not want to change my application layer. That could as pointed out also be solved by CURRENT designing a layer on top of TLS/DTLS/QUIC. But such a solution does not solve the two-party space use cases.

chat.text: Is the “problem statement” explicit somewhere? If so where. Sorry noob question.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: I think the closest thing is https://docs.google.com/document/d/1f8KDExti7Eo_LvRFIZyHKJcpVUXF0Et8vDPo5nPdRGY/edit?tab=t.0

chat.author: Marc Blanchet
chat.text: for chairs, I voted "no opinion" but my real vote was more "somewhat"

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: Without a clear problem statement how can we answer #2?

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: there is a PGP codepoint for TLS.  it was deprecated though : https://www.rfc-editor.org/info/rfc6091/

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: you'd get more than one answer

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: not sure we need that answer: there're clearly some interested IETFers who know how this stuff works, so they can come back

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: there are many problems in this space, yes

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: The question "was there at least one question presented today that the IETF is the right place to work on" could be answered, but I"m not sure it's a useful question to answer

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: I don't really think there is much point in asking more questions here.

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: right, and the proponents i guess know what to do

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: and we don't know what the work is

chat.author: Robert Moskowitz
chat.text: Yes, whatever the work is!

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text: Stephen is right, the right thing is for proponents to come back with a proposal which gets a positive answer to quesiton 1

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text: anything to do with TLS and MLS does belong in the IETF, but that isn't useful without knowing what the "any" and the "thing" are doing with each other.

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: no, if you want MLS as the handshake for the 2-party TLS protocol, you have to say why

chat.author: David Schinazi
chat.text: In space no one can hear you rekey

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: that is, what is missing from the TLS handshake that you think a new handshake would improve?

chat.author: Martin Thomson
chat.text:
David Schinazi said:

In space no one can hear you rekey

So why bother doing it?

chat.author: Eric Rescorla
chat.text:
Ye
Daniel Gillmor said:

that is, what is missing from the TLS handshake that you think a new handshake would improve?

Yes, that would be an excellent statement

chat.author: Daniel Gillmor
chat.text: if you want more yeses, try asking "are you willing to complain about the lack of clarity in the next round of problem statement?"

chat.author: Jonathan Lennox
chat.text: I don't think "are you willing to complain" is ever a question that needs to be asked at the  IETF

chat.author: John Gray
chat.text: Agreed, we need clarification of the problems and why MLS/TLS are the best way to do it.

chat.author: Ted Hardie
chat.text: Don't motivate that--describe the problem with clarity.  If that is the right answer, that will emerge.

chat.author: Harald Alvestrand
chat.text: I'm interested in the claim that MLS can do key rollover on an one-way channel. Since an one-way reliable channel is a contradiction in terms, that would require MLS key rollover to be resistent against packet loss.
